The surgeon uses forceps to push back the thickened gallbladder, which is obscured by dense adhesions. With another forceps, the surgeon carefully attempts to separate the adhesions close to the gallbladder, requiring patience due to the challenging anatomy.

The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical field, identifying the gallbladder and the duodenum. The gallbladder is positioned above the duodenum, requiring gentle manipulation to avoid injury. The surgeon approaches the fundus and body of the gallbladder, moving towards Hartmann's pouch while maintaining awareness of potential anatomical complications such as a cholecysto-duodenal fistula.

Gradually, more of the gallbladder becomes visible as adhesions are meticulously dissected away, revealing the gallbladder's serosa.

The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder, ensuring not to damage the duodenum. The falciform ligament, initially pulled towards the right side due to adhesions, is gradually repositioned as dissection progresses. The serosa of the gallbladder is taken to prevent any duodenal injury, and Hartmann's pouch becomes increasingly visible.

The surgeon identifies the 'elephant sign' where the Hartmann's pouch tapers into the cystic duct. Dissection is performed on the right lateral side to remove thick fibrous adhesions. Bipolar coagulation is used to manage potential vessels and maintain a clear surgical field, minimizing unnecessary bleeding.

The surgeon performs a retrograde dissection, working between the body of the gallbladder and the liver to create a cleavage plane in the gallbladder bed. The dissection progresses from above downwards, aiming to further separate the gallbladder from the liver tissue.

The surgeon performs a dissection to achieve the critical view of safety, clearly exposing the cystic artery, cystic duct, and part of the gallbladder from the gallbladder bed. The thickened gallbladder wall, due to multiple attacks, is visible with severe fibrosis as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon carefully navigates the fibrotic areas of the gallbladder, using a blunt grasper to find a cleavage on the left side. Due to the slippery nature of the tissue, the surgeon decides to switch to a tooth grasper for better grip. The dissection proceeds slowly to avoid damaging critical structures such as the cystic artery, bile ducts, and portal vein.

The surgeon carefully separates the gallbladder from adhesions attached to the liver, moving it right and left to free the body. The dissection progresses from the posterior side to the anterior side, with attention to bleeding and clot removal. The duodenum is pushed down, and the surgeon identifies the cystic duct, ensuring it is not mistaken for the common bile duct. Adhesions are continuously removed to clearly demonstrate the connection between the cystic duct, gallbladder, common bile duct, and common hepatic duct.

The surgeon carefully dissects posteriorly between the cystic duct and gallbladder, scraping away fascia attached on the inferior or right side. There is some bleeding observed, potentially from the posterior branch of the cystic artery within the fascia. The surgeon proceeds to clear the fascia from above to delineate the artery, ensuring anatomical structures are visible and avoiding sudden movements that could cause severe bleeding.

The surgeon continues dissection close to the gallbladder, carefully removing fascia to avoid damaging the common hepatic duct, which is often adherent below the gallbladder. Small arteries are visible, possibly supplying the cystic duct. The gallbladder is pulled up from the fossa to improve visibility and clear the quadrangle, aiding in the delineation of the cystic artery and cystic duct. Adhesions between the gallbladder and liver bed are removed, allowing safe pressure application to push the common hepatic duct down.

The surgeon uses a tooth grasper to hold the thick gallbladder while gently separating fibrous threads without using cautery, due to the risk of necrosis. Dissection proceeds slowly, alternating between posterior and anterior approaches to avoid damaging the bile duct or intestine.

The surgeon measures the gallbladder wall thickness, noting it is between one centimeter and half a centimeter, which reduces the risk of tearing with the tooth grasper. The dissection progresses superiorly and on the left side, with the fascia clearly visible. The common hepatic duct is safely positioned below. The cystic duct is now visible, and the surgeon separates it from the cystic artery posteriorly, enhancing the critical view of safety between the gallbladder and liver fossa.

The surgeon successfully achieves the critical view of safety, clearly visualizing the cystic duct and artery. A clip is applied to a small vessel, likely the posterior branch of the cystic artery, to prevent future bleeding. The surgeon then proceeds to clip the cystic duct, ensuring the clip is applied away from the junction with the common bile duct to avoid narrowing or blockage.

The surgeon carefully cuts the cystic artery after confirming its pulsation, with clips securely placed on the cystic artery and cystic duct. A small blood collection is suctioned out. The surgeon proceeds to separate the remaining gallbladder from the gallbladder fossa using cautery, noting the strong adhesions.

The surgeon carefully applies cautery close to the thick-walled gallbladder, finding the cleavage between the gallbladder and the liver while avoiding damage to surrounding liver tissue and potential venous sinuses or bile ducts.
